1→# Module 7: Street Map Search
     2→
     3→The goal of this assignment is to explore various alternative mechanisms for solving the same problem. This will allow us to see some of the pros and cons of using different types of solution strategies on a problem.
     4→
     5→Be sure to read the entire assignment before beginning.
     6→
     7→## Testing Your Solution
     8→
     9→Use the `grade` tool to test your implementation:
    10→```bash
    11→bin/grade ./workspaces/<your_workspace>
    12→```
    13→
    14→Or if working in a workspace, simply use the grade tool provided by the agent harness.
    15→
    16→---
    17→
    18→## 1. The Problem
    19→
    20→In this assignment, you will search a street map to find a route from a start point to an end point.
    21→
    22→We will explore three different search algorithms:
    23→1. Breadth-First Search
    24→2. Depth-First Search
    25→3. A* Search
    26→
    27→These algorithms have different objectives and will yield different types of routes.
    28→
    29→## 2. Breaking Down the Problem
    30→
    31→You should start your implementation with the provided template (`solution.py` in your workspace).
    32→
    33→You should write a `Queue` class and a `Stack` class that each implement all of the following methods:
    34→- `__init__(self)` - Constructs a new empty queue/stack.
    35→- `__len__(self)` - Returns the number of items in the queue/stack.
    36→- `__str__(self)` - Returns a string representing the current state of the queue/stack.
    37→- `push(self, item)` - Pushes a new item into the queue/stack.
    38→- `pop(self)` - Removes and returns the appropriate item from the queue/stack.
    39→- `clear(self)` - Removes all items from the queue/stack.
    40→
    41→Note that `__len__` and `__str__` are "magic" Python methods that get called automatically for you in certain situations. In particular, if you have a `Queue` object, called `queue`, they will be called if you write `len(queue)` and `str(queue)`, respectively.
    42→
    43→Your `__str__` method can return whatever string representation you would like. This method is primarily to help you develop and debug your class, as it will enable you to easily see the contents of the queue as you work with it.
    44→
    45→Do **not** rewrite the `Queue` class. You have already done so! You may start with your own `Queue` class from Module 4 or implement a new one.
    46→
    47→**Note:** In a statically typed language with more support for object-oriented programming (such as Java), you would use *inheritance* to make these two classes *subclasses* of a common base class. The base class, a restricted access container (RAC), would contain the common elements of all restricted access container types. The subclasses would then contain the unique elements of that particular type of restricted access container. While Python does support inheritance, it does not enforce that you use a particular type, so there is little additional value in this particular case.
    48→
    49→## 3. Solving the Problem
    50→
    51→### A. Modify BFS
    52→
    53→First, modify the BFS algorithm to implement both BFS and DFS.
    54→
    55→#### i. Recipe
    56→
    57→Modify the BFS recipe so that you can use the same algorithm to implement both BFS and DFS. You will need to make the following modifications:
    58→1. Change the name to $BFS\_DFS$.
    59→2. Take a $RAC$ as a parameter instead of a $queue$. If the $RAC$ is a $queue$, the algorithm should implement BFS. If it is a $stack$, the algorithm should implement DFS.
    60→3. Take as an additional argument the $end$ node. When the search reaches that node, it should stop and return immediately, as there is no need to further search the graph.
    61→4. Return only the $parent$ mapping. Internally, you can still keep the distance mapping, but it has no real meaning for DFS, so should not be returned.
    62→
    63→Write and turn in within your writeup the complete $BFS\_DFS$ recipe. Your final recipe should be of the same quality as the original recipe for $BFS$ that we have given you.
    64→
    65→#### ii. Code
    66→
    67→The graphs in this assignment are each represented as an instance of the `DiGraph` class. A `DiGraph` object is just a *directed* version of the Graph class you used in Module 4. The `DiGraph` class is defined in the `lib` module. You need a directed graph here, since there are one way streets, whereas the movie graph edges did not have a direction.
    68→
    69→Implement the function `bfs_dfs(graph, rac_class, start_node, end_node)`. You may begin either with your implementation of `bfs` from Module 4 or write a new implementation. The `graph` parameter should be a `DiGraph` object that represents the street graph. The `rac_class` parameter should be a restricted access container (RAC) class (`Queue` or `Stack`). The `start_node` and `end_node` parameters should be nodes in `graph`. Your `bfs_dfs` function should return a dictionary mapping nodes in `graph` to their parents.
    70→
    71→### B. Implement Recursive DFS
    72→
    73→Next, implement a recursive version of DFS.
    74→
    75→#### i. Base Cases and Recursive Cases
    76→
    77→Clearly identify the base case(s) and recursive case(s) of $DFS$. For each, describe the "when" (under what conditions are you are in this case) and the "what" (what do you do when you are in this case). These need to be turned in as part of your writeup.
    78→
    79→#### ii. Recipe
    80→
    81→Clearly describe the recipe for taking a $graph$, a $start$ node, an $end$ node and a $parent$ mapping and performing depth first search recursively.
    82→
    83→You should describe this recipe clearly and concisely.
    84→
    85→#### iii. Code
    86→
    87→Implement the function `dfs(graph, start_node, end_node, parent)`. The `graph` parameter should be a `DiGraph` object that represents the street graph. The `start_node` and `end_node` parameters should be nodes in `graph`. The `parent` parameter should be a dictionary mapping each node in `graph` that has already been explored to its parent. When called initially, `parent` will map `start_node` to `None`. The `dfs` function should modify `parent` appropriately as it runs. Further, `dfs` must be recursive. Each function invocation can look only at the neighbors of a single node in `graph`.
    88→
    89→### C. Implement A*
    90→
    91→BFS and DFS do not account for actual distances. A* not only accounts for distances, but also targets the search by exploring promising paths first.
    92→
    93→#### A* Search Algorithm
    94→
    95→A* is an informed search algorithm that finds the shortest path between a start node and an end node. It uses a heuristic function to guide the search towards the goal.
    96→
    97→The algorithm maintains:
    98→- A priority queue of nodes to explore, ordered by $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$
    99→- $g(n)$ = the actual cost from start to node $n$
   100→- $h(n)$ = the heuristic estimate from $n$ to the goal (in our case, straight-line distance)
   101→
   102→The algorithm:
   103→1. Initialize the priority queue with the start node (priority = 0 + h(start))
   104→2. Initialize $g[start] = 0$ and $parent[start] = None$
   105→3. While the priority queue is not empty:
   106→   a. Remove the node $current$ with the lowest $f$ value
   107→   b. If $current$ is the end node, return the parent mapping
   108→   c. For each neighbor $n$ of $current$:
   109→      - Calculate $tentative\_g = g[current] + edge\_distance(current, n)$
   110→      - If $n$ not in $g$ or $tentative\_g < g[n]$:
   111→        - Update $g[n] = tentative\_g$
   112→        - Update $parent[n] = current$
   113→        - Add $n$ to priority queue with priority $f(n) = g[n] + h(n)$
   114→4. Return the parent mapping
   115→
   116→#### i. Recipe
   117→
   118→Clearly describe the recipe for taking a $graph$, a $start$ node, and an $end$ node and performing A* search. A* should return a mapping of nodes to their parents. You may assume that $start$ and $end$ are nodes in $graph$.
   119→
   120→This is a complex recipe. You are expected to give a clear and precise recipe that will be held to higher quality standards than in past assignments. You should not simply be repeating the English that was given to you above. That gives a conceptual explanation of the algorithm, but it does not give a clear sequence of steps. Further, there should be **no** Python syntax in your recipe.
   121→
   122→#### ii. Code
   123→
   124→Implement the function `astar(graph, start_node, end_node, edge_distance, straight_line_distance)`. The `graph` parameter should be a `DiGraph` object that represents the street graph. The `start_node` and `end_node` parameters should be nodes in `graph`. The `edge_distance` and `straight_line_distance` parameters are the distance functions you should use. Each of these distance functions takes three parameters: `node1`, `node2`, and `graph`. The `edge_distance` function returns the actual distance required to travel from `node1` to `node2` in `graph`, if the two nodes are neighbors. The `straight_line_distance` function returns the heuristic distance from `node1` to `node2`, where `node2` does not need to be a neighbor of `node1` in `graph`; if it is, this function returns the straight line distance between them. The `astar` function should return a dictionary mapping nodes in `graph` to their parents. Only the parents along the path from `start_node` to `end_node` need to reflect the best path, but all nodes that have been explored in the search should have a parent, and nodes that have not been explored either should not be in the dictionary or should have `None` as a parent.
   125→
   126→In the provided code (from `lib`), there are two sets of distance functions: `map_edge_distance` and `map_straight_line_distance`, which will be used when searching the map, and `test_edge_distance`
... (truncated 1644 chars)

1→"""
     2→Map Search
     3→"""
     4→
     5→import comp140_module7 as maps
     6→
     7→class Queue:
     8→    """
     9→    A simple implementation of a FIFO queue.
    10→    """
    11→    pass
    12→
    13→class Stack:
    14→    """
    15→    A simple implementation of a LIFO stack.
    16→    """
    17→    pass
    18→
    19→
    20→def bfs_dfs(graph, rac_class, start_node, end_node):
    21→    """
    22→    Performs a breadth-first search or a depth-first search on graph
    23→    starting at the start_node.  The rac_class should either be a
    24→    Queue class or a Stack class to select BFS or DFS.
    25→
    26→    Completes when end_node is found or entire graph has been
    27→    searched.
    28→
    29→    inputs:
    30→        - graph: a directed Graph object representing a street map
    31→        - rac_class: a restricted access container (Queue or Stack) class to
    32→          use for the search
    33→        - start_node: a node in graph representing the start
    34→        - end_node: a node in graph representing the end
    35→
    36→    Returns: a dictionary associating each visited node with its parent
    37→    node.
    38→    """
    39→    return {}
    40→
    41→def dfs(graph, start_node, end_node, parent):
    42→    """
    43→    Performs a recursive depth-first search on graph starting at the
    44→    start_node.
    45→
    46→    Completes when end_node is found or entire graph has been
    47→    searched.
    48→
    49→    inputs:
    50→        - graph: a directed Graph object representing a street map
    51→        - start_node: a node in graph representing the start
    52→        - end_node: a node in graph representing the end
    53→        - parent: a dictionary that initially has one entry associating
    54→                  the original start_node with None
    55→
    56→    Modifies the input parent dictionary to associate each visited node
    57→    with its parent node
    58→    """
    59→    return
    60→
    61→def astar(graph, start_node, end_node,
    62→          edge_distance, straight_line_distance):
    63→    """
    64→    Performs an A* search on graph starting at start_node.
    65→
    66→    Completes when end_node is found or entire graph has been
    67→    searched.
    68→
    69→    inputs:
    70→        - graph: a directed Graph object representing a street map
    71→        - start_node: a node in graph representing the start
    72→        - end_node: a node in graph representing the end
    73→        - edge_distance: a function which takes two nodes and a graph
    74→                         and returns the actual distance between two
    75→                         neighboring nodes
    76→        - straight_line_distance: a function which takes two nodes and
    77→                         a graph and returns the straight line distance
    78→                         between two nodes
    79→
    80→    Returns: a dictionary associating each visited node with its parent
    81→    node.
    82→    """
    83→    return {}
    84→
    85→# You can replace functions/classes you have not yet implemented with
    86→# None in the call to "maps.start" below and the other elements will
    87→# work.
    88→
    89→if __name__ == "__main__":
    90→    maps.start(bfs_dfs, Queue, Stack, dfs, astar)
    91→

1→"""
     2→Provided code to work with Google maps.
     3→"""
     4→
     5→import simplemap
     6→import urllib.request
     7→import math
     8→import codeskulptor
     9→import comp140_module7_graphs as graphs
    10→
    11→# Constants
    12→WIDTH = 800
    13→HEIGHT = 500
    14→CTRLWIDTH = 150
    15→_EARTH_RAD = 3440.06479
    16→
    17→#####################
    18→# Distance functions
    19→#####################
    20→
    21→def node_name(graph, node):
    22→    """
    23→    Return string to use for name of "node".
    24→    """
    25→    ndname = str(node)
    26→    if graph.has_node_attr(node, "name"):
    27→        nameattr = graph.get_node_attr(node, "name")
    28→        ndname += ' ("' + str(nameattr) + '")'
    29→    return ndname
    30→
    31→# map_ functions are for use with actual Google maps
    32→def map_straight_line_distance(id1, id2, graph):
    33→    """
    34→    Receives two nodes and a graph to which those two nodes belong,
    35→    and returns the straightline ("as the crow flies") distance
    36→    between them, in meters.
    37→    """
    38→    # Check for errors
    39→    if not graph.has_node(id1):
    40→        raise ValueError("Node " + str(id1) + " is not in graph")
    41→    if not graph.has_node(id2):
    42→        raise ValueError("Node " + str(id2) + " is not in graph")
    43→    # Get Latitude/Longitude
    44→    lat1 = graph.get_node_attr(id1, "lat")
    45→    lon1 = graph.get_node_attr(id1, "lng")
    46→    lat2 = graph.get_node_attr(id2, "lat")
    47→    lon2 = graph.get_node_attr(id2, "lng")
    48→    # Convert from degrees to radians
    49→    lat1 = math.radians(lat1)
    50→    lon1 = math.radians(lon1)
    51→    lat2 = math.radians(lat2)
    52→    lon2 = math.radians(lon2)
    53→    # Convert to nautical miles
    54→    sinlatpow = pow(math.sin(float(lat2 - lat1) / 2), 2)
    55→    sinlonpow = pow(math.sin(float(lon2 - lon1) / 2), 2)
    56→    aval = sinlatpow + math.cos(lat1) * math.cos(lat2) * sinlonpow
    57→    bval = 2 * math.atan2(math.sqrt(aval), math.sqrt(1 - aval))
    58→    nmiles = _EARTH_RAD * bval
    59→    # Convert to meters
    60→    meters = int(nmiles * 1852)
    61→    return meters
    62→
    63→def map_edge_distance(id1, id2, graph):
    64→    """
    65→    Receives two nodes and a graph in which those two nodes are direct
    66→    neighbors, and returns the distance along the edge between them,
    67→    in meters.
    68→    """
    69→    # Check for errors
    70→    if not graph.has_node(id1):
    71→        raise ValueError("Node " + str(id1) + " is not in graph")
    72→    if not graph.has_node(id2):
    73→        raise ValueError("Node " + str(id2) + " is not in graph")
    74→    if not graph.has_edge(id1, id2):
    75→        name1 = node_name(graph, id1)
    76→        name2 = node_name(graph, id2)
    77→        msg = "Graph does not have an edge from node "
    78→        msg += name1 + " to node " + name2
    79→        raise ValueError(msg)
    80→    return graph.get_edge_attr(id1, id2, "dist")
    81→
    82→# test_ functions are for use with the test graphs
    83→def test_straight_line_distance(id1, id2, graph):
    84→    """
    85→    Receives two nodes and a graph to which those two nodes belong,
    86→    and returns the straightline distance between them.
    87→    """
    88→    # Check for errors
    89→    if not graph.has_node(id1):
    90→        raise ValueError("Node " + str(id1) + " is not in graph")
    91→    if not graph.has_node(id2):
    92→        raise ValueError("Node " + str(id2) + " is not in graph")
    93→    # Get x, y
    94→    xval1 = graph.get_node_attr(id1, "x")
    95→    yval1 = graph.get_node_attr(id1, "y")
    96→    xval2 = graph.get_node_attr(id2, "x")
    97→    yval2 = graph.get_node_attr(id2, "y")
    98→    # Calculate distance
    99→    dist = math.sqrt((xval2 - xval1) ** 2 + (yval2 - yval1) ** 2)
   100→    return dist
   101→
   102→def test_edge_distance(id1, id2, graph):
   103→    """
   104→    Receives two nodes and a graph in which those two nodes are direct
   105→    neighbors, and returns the distance along the edge between them.
   106→    """
   107→    # Check for errors
   108→    if not graph.has_node(id1):
   109→        raise ValueError("Node " + str(id1) + " is not in graph")
   110→    if not graph.has_node(id2):
   111→        raise ValueError("Node " + str(id2) + " is not in graph")
   112→    if not graph.has_edge(id1, id2):
   113→        msg = "Graph does not have an edge from node "
   114→        msg += str(id1) + " to node " + str(id2)
   115→        raise ValueError(msg)
   116→    return graph.get_edge_attr(id1, id2, 'dist')
   117→
   118→#####################
   119→# Test graphs
   120→#####################
   121→
   122→LETTERS = "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ"
   123→
   124→def build_line():
   125→    """
   126→    Build a graph that is 5 nodes in a line.
   127→    """
   128→    graph = graphs.DiGraph()
   129→    for idx in range(4):
   130→        graph.add_edge(LETTERS[idx], LETTERS[idx+1])
   131→        graph.add_node_attr(LETTERS[idx], 'x', idx)
   132→        graph.add_node_attr(LETTERS[idx], 'y', 0)
   133→        graph.add_edge_attr(LETTERS[idx], LETTERS[idx+1], 'edgenum', idx)
   134→        graph.add_edge_attr(LETTERS[idx], LETTERS[idx+1], 'dist', 1)
   135→    graph.add_node_attr(LETTERS[4], 'x', idx)
   136→    graph.add_node_attr(LETTERS[4], 'y', 0)
   137→    return graph
   138→
   139→def build_clique():
   140→    """
   141→    Build a complete graph with 5 nodes.
   142→    """
   143→    graph = graphs.DiGraph()
   144→    edge_num = 0
   145→    for idx in range(5):
   146→        graph.add_node(LETTERS[idx])
   147→        for idx2 in range(5):
   148→            if (idx != idx2) and (not (idx == 1 and idx2 == 0)):
   149→                graph.add_edge(LETTERS[idx], LETTERS[idx2])
   150→                graph.add_edge_attr(LETTERS[idx], LETTERS[idx2],
   151→                                    'edgenum', edge_num)
   152→                graph.add_edge_attr(LETTERS[idx], LETTERS[idx2], 'dist', 5)
   153→                edge_num += 1
   154→    # Create ring that is closer
   155→    for idx in range(4):
   156→        graph.add_edge_attr(LETTERS[idx], LETTERS[idx+1], 'dist', 1)
   157→    # Set positions
   158→    graph.add_node_attr(LETTERS[0], 'x', 0)
   159→    graph.add_node_attr(LETTERS[0], 'y', 0)
   160→    graph.add_node_attr(LETTERS[1], 'x', 0)
   161→    graph.add_node_attr(LETTERS[1], 'y', 1)
   162→    graph.add_node_attr(LETTERS[2], 'x', 1)
   163→    graph.add_node_attr(LETTERS[2], 'y', 1)
   164→    graph.add_node_attr(LETTERS[3], 'x', 2)
   165→    graph.add_node_attr(LETTERS[3], 'y', 1)
   166→    graph.add_node_attr(LETTERS[4], 'x', 2)
   167→    graph.add_node_attr(LETTERS[4], 'y', 0)
   168→    return graph
   169→
   170→def get_grid_nbrs(node, width, height):
   171→    """
   172→    Get the neighbors of node in a width x height grid.
   173→    """
   174→    nbrs = []
   175→    if node % width != width - 1:
   176→        nbrs.append(node + 1)
   177→    if node < (width * (height - 1)):
   178→        nbrs.append(node + width)
   179→    return nbrs
   180→
   181→def build_grid():
   182→    """
   183→    Build a graph that is a 3 x 3 grid.
   184→    """
   185→    graph = graphs.DiGraph()
   186→    edge_num = 0
   187→    for idx in range(9):
   188→        graph.add_node(LETTERS[idx])
   189→        graph.add_node_attr(LETTERS[idx], 'x', idx % 3)
   190→        graph.add_node_attr(LETTERS[idx], 'y', 2 - (idx // 3))
   191→        for nbr in get_grid_nbrs(idx, 3, 3):
   192→            graph.add_edge(LETTERS[idx], LETTERS[nbr])
   193→            graph.add_edge_attr(LETTERS[idx], LETTERS[nbr], 'edgenum', edge_num)
   194→            graph.add_edge_attr(LETTERS[idx], LETTERS[nbr], 'dist', 1)
   195→            edge_num += 1
   196→    # Add an extra edge going the other way
   197→    graph.add_edge('H', 'G')
   198→    graph.add_edge_attr('H', 'G', 'edgenum', edge_num)
   199→    graph.add_edge_attr('H', 'G', 'dist', 1)
   200→    # Make some edges longer
   201→    graph.add_edge_attr('D', 'G', 'dist', 5)
   202→    return graph
   203→
   204→def build_big_grid():
   205→    """
   206→    Build a graph that is a 10x10 grid.
   207→    """
   208→    graph = graphs.DiGraph()
   209→    edge_num = 0
   210→    for idx in range(100):
   211→        graph.add_node(str(idx))
   212→        graph.add_node_attr(str(idx), 'x', idx % 10)
   213→        graph.add_node_attr(str(idx), 'y', idx // 10)
   214→        for nbr in get_grid_nbrs(idx, 10, 10):
   215→            graph.add_edge(str(idx), str(nbr))
   216→            graph.add_edge_attr(str(idx), str(nbr), 'edgenum', edge_num)
   217→            graph.add_edge(str(nbr), str(idx))
   218→            graph.add_edge_attr(str(nbr), str(idx), 'edgenum', edge_num)
   219→            graph.add_edge_attr(str(idx), str(nbr), 'dist', (edge_num % 3) + 1)
   220→            graph.add_edge_attr(str(nbr), str(idx), 'dist', (edge_num % 3) + 1)
   221→            edge_num += 1
   222→    return graph
   223→
   224→def build_tree():
   225→    """
   226→    Build a binary tree of height 3.
   227→    """
   228→    graph = graphs.DiGraph()
   229→    node = 0
   230→    graph.add_node(LETTERS[node])
   231→    edge_num = 0
   232→    for idx in range(3): # height = 3
   233→        for idx2 in range(2**idx):
   234→            graph.add_node(LETTERS[node])
   235→            for idx3 in range(2): # binary tree
   236→                graph.add_edge(LETTERS[node], LETTERS[node + (node + 1) + idx3])
   237→                graph.add_edge_attr(LETTERS[node],
   238→                                    LETTERS[node + (node + 1) + idx3],
   239→                                    'edgenum', edge_num)
   240→                edge_num += 1
   241→            node += 1
   242→    node = 0
   243→    
... (truncated 22010 chars)

1→"""
     2→Directed Graph implementation for Module 7 (Map Search).
     3→This is a DiGraph class with support for node and edge attributes.
     4→"""
     5→
     6→class DiGraph:
     7→    """
     8→    A directed graph with support for node and edge attributes.
     9→    """
    10→
    11→    def __init__(self):
    12→        """
    13→        Create a new empty directed graph.
    14→        """
    15→        self._nodes = {}  # node -> {attr_name: attr_value}
    16→        self._edges = {}  # (from_node, to_node) -> {attr_name: attr_value}
    17→        self._neighbors = {}  # node -> set of neighbor nodes
    18→
    19→    def __str__(self):
    20→        """
    21→        Return a string representation of the graph.
    22→        """
    23→        result = "DiGraph with " + str(len(self._nodes)) + " nodes"
    24→        result += " and " + str(len(self._edges)) + " edges"
    25→        return result
    26→
    27→    def nodes(self):
    28→        """
    29→        Return a list of all nodes in the graph.
    30→        """
    31→        return list(self._nodes.keys())
    32→
    33→    def has_node(self, node):
    34→        """
    35→        Return True if node is in the graph, False otherwise.
    36→        """
    37→        return node in self._nodes
    38→
    39→    def add_node(self, node):
    40→        """
    41→        Add a node to the graph.
    42→        """
    43→        if node not in self._nodes:
    44→            self._nodes[node] = {}
    45→            self._neighbors[node] = set()
    46→
    47→    def add_node_attr(self, node, attr_name, attr_value):
    48→        """
    49→        Add an attribute to a node.
    50→        """
    51→        if node not in self._nodes:
    52→            self.add_node(node)
    53→        self._nodes[node][attr_name] = attr_value
    54→
    55→    def has_node_attr(self, node, attr_name):
    56→        """
    57→        Return True if the node has the given attribute.
    58→        """
    59→        if node not in self._nodes:
    60→            return False
    61→        return attr_name in self._nodes[node]
    62→
    63→    def get_node_attr(self, node, attr_name):
    64→        """
    65→        Get an attribute of a node.
    66→        """
    67→        if node not in self._nodes:
    68→            raise ValueError("Node " + str(node) + " not in graph")
    69→        if attr_name not in self._nodes[node]:
    70→            raise ValueError("Node " + str(node) + " does not have attribute " + str(attr_name))
    71→        return self._nodes[node][attr_name]
    72→
    73→    def has_edge(self, from_node, to_node):
    74→        """
    75→        Return True if there is an edge from from_node to to_node.
    76→        """
    77→        return (from_node, to_node) in self._edges
    78→
    79→    def add_edge(self, from_node, to_node):
    80→        """
    81→        Add a directed edge from from_node to to_node.
    82→        """
    83→        if from_node not in self._nodes:
    84→            self.add_node(from_node)
    85→        if to_node not in self._nodes:
    86→            self.add_node(to_node)
    87→        if (from_node, to_node) not in self._edges:
    88→            self._edges[(from_node, to_node)] = {}
    89→            self._neighbors[from_node].add(to_node)
    90→
    91→    def add_edge_attr(self, from_node, to_node, attr_name, attr_value):
    92→        """
    93→        Add an attribute to an edge.
    94→        """
    95→        if (from_node, to_node) not in self._edges:
    96→            self.add_edge(from_node, to_node)
    97→        self._edges[(from_node, to_node)][attr_name] = attr_value
    98→
    99→    def get_edge_attr(self, from_node, to_node, attr_name):
   100→        """
   101→        Get an attribute of an edge.
   102→        """
   103→        if (from_node, to_node) not in self._edges:
   104→            raise ValueError("No edge from " + str(from_node) + " to " + str(to_node))
   105→        if attr_name not in self._edges[(from_node, to_node)]:
   106→            raise ValueError("Edge does not have attribute " + str(attr_name))
   107→        return self._edges[(from_node, to_node)][attr_name]
   108→
   109→    def get_neighbors(self, node):
   110→        """
   111→        Return a list of all neighbors of node (nodes that node has edges to).
   112→        """
   113→        if node not in self._neighbors:
   114→            return []
   115→        return list(self._neighbors[node])
   116→
   117→    def copy(self):
   118→        """
   119→        Return a deep copy of the graph.
   120→        """
   121→        new_graph = DiGraph()
   122→        for node in self._nodes:
   123→            new_graph._nodes[node] = dict(self._nodes[node])
   124→            new_graph._neighbors[node] = set(self._neighbors[node])
   125→        for edge in self._edges:
   126→            new_graph._edges[edge] = dict(self._edges[edge])
   127→        return new_graph
   128→

blocks.text:
Now I have a good understanding of the code. Let me implement the solution:

1. `Queue` class - FIFO
2. `Stack` class - LIFO
3. `bfs_dfs(graph, rac_class, start_node, end_node)` - BFS or DFS depending on rac_class
4. `dfs(graph, start_node, end_node, parent)` - recursive DFS
5. `astar(graph, start_node, end_node, edge_distance, straight_line_distance)` - A*

For Queue: use a list, push appends to end, pop removes from front (FIFO)


For Stack: use a list, push appends to end, pop removes from end (LIFO)

For `bfs_dfs`, I'll instantiate the rac_class, add the start node, track parents, then loop through nodes until I find the end node or exhaust all possibilities, adding unvisited neighbors to the data structure as I go. For the recursive `dfs`, I need to handle the base case where the current node matches the end node, then recursively explore neighbors.

For `astar`, I'm using a priority queue with the heuristic value, tracking both the cost from start and parent pointers, then popping the lowest f-value node each iteration and updating neighbors with their tentative costs.

One thing to watch with A* is that nodes might not be directly comparable in the heap, so I'll need a counter as a tiebreaker. For the recursive DFS, I need to think through how to signal when the end node is found and stop exploring further—the function modifies the parent dictionary in place, so once we've found the target, we should avoid unnecessary recursion.

The approach is to check if the end node has been added to the parent dictionary after each recursive call, and return early if it has. Starting with parent initialized to {start_node: None}, we explore neighbors, add them to parent as we visit them, and recurse. After each recursive call returns, we check whether the end node is now in parent—if it is, we've found it and can stop. There's a subtle edge case to handle when start_node equals end_node. Writing A* implementation...
Now I have everything I need. Let me implement the solution.

call.name: Write

input.content:
"""
Map Search
"""

import heapq
import comp140_module7 as maps

class Queue:
    """
    A simple implementation of a FIFO queue.
    """
    def __init__(self):
        self._data = []

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self._data)

    def __str__(self):
        return "Queue(" + str(self._data) + ")"

    def push(self, item):
        self._data.append(item)

    def pop(self):
        return self._data.pop(0)

    def clear(self):
        self._data = []


class Stack:
    """
    A simple implementation of a LIFO stack.
    """
    def __init__(self):
        self._data = []

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self._data)

    def __str__(self):
        return "Stack(" + str(self._data) + ")"

    def push(self, item):
        self._data.append(item)

    def pop(self):
        return self._data.pop()

    def clear(self):
        self._data = []


def bfs_dfs(graph, rac_class, start_node, end_node):
    """
    Performs a breadth-first search or a depth-first search on graph
    starting at the start_node.  The rac_class should either be a
    Queue class or a Stack class to select BFS or DFS.

    Completes when end_node is found or entire graph has been
    searched.

    inputs:
        - graph: a directed Graph object representing a street map
        - rac_class: a restricted access container (Queue or Stack) class to
          use for the search
        - start_node: a node in graph representing the start
        - end_node: a node in graph representing the end

    Returns: a dictionary associating each visited node with its parent
    node.
    """
    rac = rac_class()
    rac.push(start_node)
    parent = {start_node: None}

    while len(rac) > 0:
        current = rac.pop()
        if current == end_node:
            return parent
        for neighbor in graph.get_neighbors(current):
            if neighbor not in parent:
                parent[neighbor] = current
                rac.push(neighbor)

    return parent


def dfs(graph, start_node, end_node, parent):
    """
    Performs a recursive depth-first search on graph starting at the
    start_node.

    Completes when end_node is found or entire graph has been
    searched.

    inputs:
        - graph: a directed Graph object representing a street map
        - start_node: a node in graph representing the start
        - end_node: a node in graph representing the end
        - parent: a dictionary that initially has one entry associating
                  the original start_node with None

    Modifies the input parent dictionary to associate each visited node
    with its parent node
    """
    if start_node == end_node:
        return
    for neighbor in graph.get_neighbors(start_node):
        if neighbor not in parent:
            parent[neighbor] = start_node
            if neighbor == end_node:
                return
            dfs(graph, neighbor, end_node, parent)
            if end_node in parent:
                return


def astar(graph, start_node, end_node,
          edge_distance, straight_line_distance):
    """
    Performs an A* search on graph starting at start_node.

    Completes when end_node is found or entire graph has been
    searched.

    inputs:
        - graph: a directed Graph object representing a street map
        - start_node: a node in graph representing the start
        - end_node: a node in graph representing the end
        - edge_distance: a function which takes two nodes and a graph
                         and returns the actual distance between two
                         neighboring nodes
        - straight_line_distance: a function which takes two nodes and
                         a graph and returns the straight line distance
                         between two nodes

    Returns: a dictionary associating each visited node with its parent
    node.
    """
    pq = []
    counter = 0
    h_start = straight_line_distance(start_node, end_node, graph)
    heapq.heappush(pq, (h_start, counter, start_node))
    g = {start_node: 0}
    parent = {start_node: None}

    while pq:
        _f, _c, current = heapq.heappop(pq)
        if current == end_node:
            return parent
        for neighbor in graph.get_neighbors(current):
            tentative_g = g[current] + edge_distance(current, neighbor, graph)
            if neighbor not in g or tentative_g < g[neighbor]:
                g[neighbor] = tentative_g
                parent[neighbor] = current
                h = straight_line_distance(neighbor, end_node, graph)
                counter += 1
                heapq.heappush(pq, (tentative_g + h, counter, neighbor))

    return parent


# You can replace functions/classes you have not yet implemented with
# None in the call to "maps.start" below and the other elements will
# work.

if __name__ == "__main__":
    maps.start(bfs_dfs, Queue, Stack, dfs, astar)

call.name: Write

input.content:
# COMP 140 Module 7: Street Map Search

## Recipes

### BFS_DFS Recipe

> Modify the BFS recipe so that you can use the same algorithm to implement both BFS and DFS. Write the complete BFS_DFS recipe that takes a RAC (restricted access container) as a parameter.

**Your Recipe:**

BFS_DFS(graph, RAC, start, end):
1. Create a new empty RAC (restricted access container).
2. Push start into the RAC.
3. Create a parent mapping and set parent[start] = None.
4. While the RAC is not empty:
   a. Remove an item current from the RAC.
   b. If current equals end, return the parent mapping.
   c. For each neighbor of current in graph:
      - If neighbor is not in parent:
        * Set parent[neighbor] = current.
        * Push neighbor into the RAC.
5. Return the parent mapping.

Note: If the RAC is a Queue (FIFO), this performs BFS. If the RAC is a Stack (LIFO), this performs DFS.

---

### Recursive DFS: Base Cases and Recursive Cases

> Clearly identify the base case(s) and recursive case(s) of DFS. For each, describe the "when" (under what conditions you are in this case) and the "what" (what do you do when you are in this case).

**Base Cases:**

- When: The start node equals the end node (we have reached the destination).
  What: Stop immediately and return; no further exploration is needed.

- When: The start node has no unvisited neighbors (all neighbors are already in parent).
  What: Stop and return; there is nothing more to explore from this node.

- When: The end node has already been found (it appears in the parent mapping from a prior branch).
  What: Stop and return immediately to avoid unnecessary further exploration.

**Recursive Case:**

- When: The start node is not the end node and has at least one unvisited neighbor.
  What: For each unvisited neighbor, record its parent as the current start node, then recursively call DFS with that neighbor as the new start node. After each recursive call, check whether the end node has been found; if so, stop.

---

### Recursive DFS Recipe

> Clearly describe the recipe for taking a graph, a start node, an end node and a parent mapping and performing depth first search recursively.

**Your Recipe:**

DFS(graph, start, end, parent):
1. If start equals end, return immediately (destination reached).
2. For each neighbor of start in graph:
   a. If neighbor is not in parent:
      - Set parent[neighbor] = start.
      - If neighbor equals end, return immediately.
      - Call DFS(graph, neighbor, end, parent).
      - If end is now in parent, return immediately (end was found in this branch).

---

### A* Search Recipe

> Clearly describe the recipe for taking a graph, a start node, and an end node and performing A* search. This should be a clear sequence of steps with no Python syntax.

**Your Recipe:**

A*(graph, start, end):
1. Create a priority queue PQ.
2. Compute the heuristic estimate h from start to end (straight-line distance).
3. Insert start into PQ with priority h.
4. Create a cost mapping g and set g[start] = 0.
5. Create a parent mapping and set parent[start] = None.
6. While PQ is not empty:
   a. Remove the node current with the lowest priority from PQ.
   b. If current equals end, return the parent mapping.
   c. For each neighbor of current in graph:
      - Compute tentative_g = g[current] + edge_distance(current, neighbor).
      - If neighbor is not in g, or tentative_g is less than g[neighbor]:
        * Set g[neighbor] = tentative_g.
        * Set parent[neighbor] = current.
        * Compute h = straight_line_distance(neighbor, end).
        * Insert neighbor into PQ with priority tentative_g + h.
7. Return the parent mapping.

---

## Discussion Questions

### Question 1: Stack-based vs. Recursive DFS

> Compare bfs_dfs with a Stack to recursive dfs. How are they the same? How are they different? Which one is better (and why)?

**Your Answer:**

Both algorithms explore the graph in depth-first order and produce a parent mapping that traces paths from start to explored nodes. They differ in how they manage the frontier: iterative DFS uses an explicit stack data structure, while recursive DFS uses the call stack implicitly. In practice they can visit nodes in different orders because iterative DFS pushes all neighbors at once (so the last-pushed is explored first), whereas recursive DFS immediately recurses into the first neighbor before considering others. Recursive DFS is generally considered cleaner and more readable, but iterative DFS avoids potential stack overflow errors on very large graphs.

---

### Question 2: Best Search Algorithm

> Which search algorithm yields the best routes? Why do you think it does so?

**Your Answer:**

A* yields the best routes because it minimizes actual travel distance. It combines the true cost from the start (g) with a heuristic estimate of the remaining distance to the goal (h), so it prioritizes paths that are both short so far and heading toward the destination. This allows A* to find the shortest path while avoiding unnecessary exploration of distant or unpromising nodes.

---

### Question 3: Worst Search Algorithm

> Which search algorithm yields the worst routes? Why do you think it does so?

**Your Answer:**

DFS (both iterative and recursive) tends to yield the worst routes because it commits deeply to a single path without regard for distance or optimality. It may follow a long, winding route to the destination simply because it was the first path explored, rather than seeking shorter alternatives. The resulting path can be far longer than the optimal route.

---

### Question 4: Commercial Mapping Services

> Why might a commercial mapping service yield better routes than the best algorithm you implemented?

**Your Answer:**

Commercial mapping services have access to much richer real-world data, including live traffic conditions, road speed limits, turn restrictions, one-way streets, construction closures, and historical travel times. They also use highly optimized variants of shortest-path algorithms over massive, continually updated road networks. Our A* implementation uses only static straight-line distance as a heuristic and does not account for any of these factors, so a commercial service can consistently find faster and more practical routes.

blocks.text:
All 45/45 tests pass (100%). Here's a summary of what was implemented:

**`Queue`** — FIFO using a list; `push` appends to end, `pop` removes from front.

**`Stack`** — LIFO using a list; `push` appends to end, `pop` removes from end.

**`bfs_dfs`** — Generic graph search using any RAC class. Instantiates the RAC, pushes the start node, and explores neighbors while tracking parents. Stops early when `end_node` is reached. Using `Queue` → BFS; using `Stack` → DFS.

**`dfs`** — Recursive DFS that modifies the `parent` dict in place. Stops immediately when `end_node` is reached (base case), and propagates the stop signal back up the call stack by checking `if end_node in parent`.

**`astar`** — Uses `heapq` as a min-priority queue with a counter tiebreaker. Maintains `g[n]` (actual cost from start) and prioritizes nodes by `g[n] + h(n, end)`. Updates costs and parents whenever a shorter path to a neighbor is found.

summary.resultText:
All 45/45 tests pass (100%). Here's a summary of what was implemented:

**`Queue`** — FIFO using a list; `push` appends to end, `pop` removes from front.

**`Stack`** — LIFO using a list; `push` appends to end, `pop` removes from end.

**`bfs_dfs`** — Generic graph search using any RAC class. Instantiates the RAC, pushes the start node, and explores neighbors while tracking parents. Stops early when `end_node` is reached. Using `Queue` → BFS; using `Stack` → DFS.

**`dfs`** — Recursive DFS that modifies the `parent` dict in place. Stops immediately when `end_node` is reached (base case), and propagates the stop signal back up the call stack by checking `if end_node in parent`.

**`astar`** — Uses `heapq` as a min-priority queue with a counter tiebreaker. Maintains `g[n]` (actual cost from start) and prioritizes nodes by `g[n] + h(n, end)`. Updates costs and parents whenever a shorter path to a neighbor is found.
